questions.question: A $5.00\text{ kg}$ ball accelerates from rest at $2.00\text{ m/s}^2$ for $1.50\text{ s}$. Calculate its momentum after the acceleration.
A $3.00\text{ kg}$ cart is pushed from rest with a constant force of $12.0\text{ N}$ for $4.00\text{ s}$. What is its final momentum?
A $2.00\text{ kg}$ object starts from rest and reaches a final momentum of $30.0\text{ kg m/s}$ under a constant net force of $10.0\text{ N}$. How long did the force act?
An object accelerates from rest at $3.00\text{ m/s}^2$ for $2.50\text{ s}$ and reaches a final momentum of $45.0\text{ kg m/s}$. What is its mass?
A $4.00\text{ kg}$ ball changes its velocity from $2.00\text{ m/s}$ to $8.00\text{ m/s}$ in $3.00\text{ s}$. What average force acted on it?
A $2.50\text{ kg}$ object has a momentum of $20.0\text{ kg m/s}$ after accelerating for $4.00\text{ s}$ from rest. What was its acceleration?
A $6.00\text{ kg}$ cart experiences a constant force of $15.0\text{ N}$ for $2.00\text{ s}$, resulting in a final momentum of $78.0\text{ kg m/s}$. What was its initial velocity?
A $10.0\text{ kg}$ object moving at $5.00\text{ m/s}$ experiences an opposing force of $20.0\text{ N}$ for $1.50\text{ s}$. What is its final momentum?
questions.explanation: $p_{initial} = 10.0 \times 5.00 = 50.0\text{ kg m/s}$. $\Delta p = -20.0 \times 1.50 = -30.0\text{ kg m/s}$. $p_{final} = 50.0 - 30.0 = 20.0\text{ kg m/s}$.

questions.question: A force of $24.0\text{ N}$ acts on an object for $3.00\text{ s}$, changing its velocity from $2.00\text{ m/s}$ to $14.0\text{ m/s}$. What is the mass of the object?
A $5.00\text{ kg}$ cart with an initial momentum of $60.0\text{ kg m/s}$ is slowed by a friction force of $8.00\text{ N}$ for $2.50\text{ s}$. What is its final velocity?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the connections between force, acceleration, time, and momentum!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review using $F \times t = \Delta p$ as a shortcut for finding momentum change.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $v = u + at$, $p = mv$, and $F = \Delta p / t$.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: $F = ma$, $v = u + at$, $p = mv$, and the impulse-momentum relationship $F \times t = \Delta p$.
